speaker: interviewer
text: Tape 5, December, 17, 1996, with Lou Joseph in Des Plaines, Illinois, being interviewed by Leah Shifrin Averick.

speaker: interviewee
text: I want to continue with the life in the camp and the day that there was an air-- the bomber formations, US bomber formation, had flown over the camp and had attacked Magdeburg. And we heard some-- later, during the day, on the return flight to the-- to France or to England, that we heard that one of the planes had-- motor problems, engine problems, and was coming down. But it actually landed on-- on the-- on the runway, which was perfect. It was a perfect fit-- on one the-- one of the runways. And it was a silver-looking Mustang, a fighter plane-- long-range fighter-- US fighter plane. And we didn't-- we only learned later that the pilot was-- was you know, captured, surrendered to the German Air Force personnel there. And we did not see the pilot. But we were [INAUDIBLE]. And that was something really totally unexpected and unthinkable, really, for people in Germany, that the pilot was black-- had been black. It must have been-- it must have been one-- one of those people from that-- this black division of pilots. They just had a movie about them recently, I think. I can't think. They-- have a-- there's still some of these veterans livin'. So that-- that really must-- must have made quite an impression on the Germans. They didn't expect to find a black pilot there for you know--

speaker: interviewer
text: What did it do to you when you heard?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well when we heard it, we were all laughing. Because I know that in the German way of thinkin', that would be-- you know, they-- they would be considered as bad as Jews, or worse. You know, they would be called [GERMAN] because they-- they are-- you know, they're just not-- the racist--

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you translate [GERMAN]?

speaker: interviewee
text: [GERMAN] ?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah, how would you translate that?

speaker: interviewee
text: [GERMAN] were subhumans. Yeah, that's-- that's the word that they have used-- were used for Jews and gypsies and others. But I mean, this you know, was unthinkable by German terms to think that they would be honorable of a-- you know, they would never use-- the German army never used them. And you couldn't think that the US Army did, because they're not familiar with life in the-- in the United States. You know, so that was-- so in any case, that was an event, again, that made us feel good. Because we saw, gee, we're drawing toward the close or the end. And it's obvious-- I mean, Germany is not going to win this war. I mean, they're-- they're destroying everything. This airfield-- those jets-- jets never returned. They couldn't return, because they had systematically, completely laid rows of you know, bombs across-- they you know, dropped them across the airfields in all the-- in so-- in so many areas that it was impossible to land for any plane or take off. The runways were totally destroyed. So actually, our-- our work was-- was finished. I mean, there was nothing there. Although they had said we were going to you know, immediately go and trying to rebuild everything. That was like the-- it was the end of March, I think. It was like, March 31st or something like this.

speaker: interviewer
text: 1940 [INAUDIBLE]?

speaker: interviewee
text: 1945, yeah. And March 31st. And then after that, yeah, they made us-- yeah, they ordered us to clean up the next day and try and-- but there was really hardly anything to do. The destruction was so-- massive that there was no way that the-- it was inhumanly to even think of you know, to-- rebuild this airfield. And then it became even more intriguing, because for the first time, when we were laying on our-- sleeping our barracks, and we were-- would be awakened by distant artillery fire comin' in from the west. And that was the Allied armies-- in this case, the American armies, I think.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, we-- oh, we knew that. Sure, we knew that. We knew they--

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you know? How--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we had-- they had radio. And they were-- the guards had radio. And I think we might have even had a radio in the-- it was a-- it was not permitted. We might have even had a radio in one of the barracks. But I don't know. We just knew. I mean, it was-- just standard reason that we-- the airfield was destroyed. Allied troops are movin' in, you know have-- have crossed the Rhine River, and are now you know, invading the-- actually the territory of Germany. And the first night, I-- it was almost-- it was just a grumble, a rumbling noise that you heard in the distant at night. And then we got out of the barracks and looked toward the-- look toward the west. And we could see the light flickering in the-- on the horizon. And so I said, gee, it's only a-- it's only going to be a couple more days before those troops arrive here, and we're going to be free people. You know, we're going to be like normal-- we're going to be normal people again.

speaker: interviewer
text: And it didn't-- so every day, now a very optimistic-- optimistic mood swept the whole camp. I mean, it was unbelievable. Everybody said-- even the guards-- even the guards were extremely friendly. They weren't interested in doing too much, either. Because it looked-- it was senseless. I mean, everything was destroyed. And you hear the enemy artillery fire in the distance. What are you going to do? I mean, they're-- if they're already on German grounds, and they're coming, we're in the center of Germany, at this [GERMAN], you know? That's not a good sign, so-- for them. So they had to-- nobody-- the last few days went without you know, no-- any incidents of brutal-- well, there was never really any serious incident of brutality. But they-- they even became more relaxed and friendly, because they were thinking now, what-- what are they going to do, if those troops arrive here? And they're going to be all made prisoners, because they're paramilitary, or men in uniforms. You know, they're considered to be a military personnel. So then-- I think it was August-- March-- no, it was April now. It was April 12th-- April 11th or 12th, 1945, when they decided that that fire had become-- the artillery fire had become much stronger. In fact, on April 12th or 13-- I forget now. April 11th? I'm not quite sure the exact date, but it was around that time. Suddenly, we could hear small arms fire. It sounded like machine gun fire and so forth. Now, if you can hear machine gun fire and rifle shot fire, you know those troops are not very far from you.

speaker: interviewee
text: And that day, they decided to-- the guards decided, and the order came through from the SS major-- the camp has to be evacuated. There was only one place left to evacuate. All the western part of Germany was already occupied. Bavaria was the only place that was the-- that was to the southeast. So the order comes out-- everybody get ready. They're going to put us on-- on trucks. And we're going to-- we're going to be taken. We're going to start the journey to Bavaria. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: And the SS man kept coming?

speaker: interviewee
text: The SS-- no, no. That was not the SS man. That was the SS major who gave the order. But there were no SS men there. There were, again, the people from the Organisation Todt.

speaker: interviewer
text: I understand, but did the SS man come daily, as he used to, at the beginning of your incarceration?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, he came-- he came daily, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: He came daily?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I think he came that day. And he says, and the camp is going to be evacuated. And he again made some sinister comments about, where you going go-- where you going to be-- where you are going to now, there's not going to be no return, you know, saying that maybe they're going to shoot us there. So anyway, we were not eager to-- to evacuate and get on the trucks. And here comes a totally new, a changing situation. Just as the-- oh, the guards order us-- the OT guards order us to-- they had their families laying-- living around there, in houses, near the air-- airfield, like a compound there. Ordered us to take all their house good-- household goods and load them on the trucks. They wanted to take everything-- their household goods plus us-- down there. There wasn't enough room, of course, on the trucks.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we started loading the trucks, when suddenly Mustang fighter planes which appear on the horizon, and now they're flying real low. They see this. They consider this an army convoy, a German army convoy. That's a target. So they start shooting like crazy. Everybody, everybody-- but I say everybody-- scrambles for safety, all over the place. I mean, they're-- you know, the guards leaving, letting the trucks half-- half-loaded. Everybody's giong. Nobody pays attention to us. We're running toward-- there were forests around, surrounding us, I said-- like I said before. There was no barbed wire. There wasn't no walls. So it was all open territory. So everybody moves back into the forest. All our people runnin'-- runnin' toward the forest and laying down. They're coming-- they're constantly coming down, strafing with their machine guns and cannons-- all everything. You know, I mean, we would have been killed if we stay-- stayed there, you know, and loading the trucks. So we're running over. And I looked at the-- and this time, I was with this fellow from-- that I later-- you know, we-- both of us-- Kurt Kirmreuther-- What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Kirmreuther-- K-I-R-M-R-E-U-T-H-E-R. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And his first name was?

speaker: interviewee
text: Kurt-- K-U-R-T. That's a-- yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Another friend of yours?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, he was-- actually, he was a better friend than-- he was a closer friend than this other kid. We just happened to be-- on the day of the bombing, we happened to be together on the same work detail. So I felt like almost like I was a little-- I was older a couple of years. I felt like I protected-- I have to protect-- you know, be a protector to him. So I-- Kurt-- Kurt and I were seeing that. We're laying. We ran away together. And I said--

speaker: interviewer
text: You ran away to the--

speaker: interviewee
text: Toward the forest. And I-- as I say, we're laying-- you know, we're lying, crouching down, because we-- you know, the attack still goes on. And anything-- you know, any-- you would be killed, probably, in the line of-- you know, in this attack.

speaker: interviewer
text: So here is what happens. I'm looking around. And I-- suddenly, I notice the-- I push Kurt. I says, did you see that? He says, that's some of our guys. Nobody is paying attention to them. They're disappearing into the forest. I said-- you know, and we see little groups of our guys going into the-- disappearing into the forest. I said, we ought to do that, too. Because this is our chance. Forget about the barracks. Forget about the few things that we have in the barracks. Just let's get away from it, before they-- before the attack-- before the air raid is over and they load us on the trucks. And you remember what the major said? So we're doing the same thing. We're creeping back into the forest. And as soon as we hit the edge of the forest, we go father and farther into the forest. About an hour later, the air raid is over. And they're finally getting-- they're finally catching on. Who's they?

speaker: interviewee
text: The OT-- the Organisation Todt people. And now the major has [INAUDIBLE]-- he has seen this whole thing has gotten out of control with the-- with the inmates, with the camp. They all have fled into surrounding for-- you know, this-- it's very obvious. There's nobody left in the-- of the group. They must have all taken off, you know. So he brings back-- he comes back within an SS detail. We didn't know that. We didn't know that at the time. But we're we're laying. We're-- we're resting from the running and the-- and sittin' down in the forest. And suddenly, I heard the sound of a motorcycle comin'.

speaker: interviewer
text: And sure enough, a motorcycle with a [? bicar, ?] with two SS soldiers on there-- they go in through the forest. And only a motorcycle can go through the thick forest there. [INAUDIBLE] It couldn't be a-- it couldn't have been a car, you know? And they're looking for people like us, you know? So we're both laying the-- you know, down on the ground, and pressing down, and covered by bushes and everything, these real thick bushes. So they-- they pass us by. But I think where if somebody had sneezed or you know, coughed, they might [LAUGHS] have caught onto us. But fortunately, none of these things happened. So we get-- we get out. After they passed, we start running through the forest again. And we come out. On the other side of the forest, there's a community. And I forget what the name-- a village. We come out there. And suddenly, we see people there, you know, residents from that village. And we were both getting worried. He says, they know about our camp. They know about us. And what's going to happen? And here, to our surprise, they come over. And they smile at us. And they says, you're from the camp, aren't you, from camp Zerbst. I said-- [GERMAN] Zerbst? I said, yes. What did they say? What does that--

speaker: interviewee
text: [GERMAN]-- that's the German word for forced labor camp. You're from-- from Zerbst? He says, yes. And we were, you know, a little reluctant. He says, well, we know the war is over. He says, it's only a matter of time. He says, we'll-- we'll give you some regular clothes, because we still have had our work outfits on. That was not a good idea. So they gave us some-- they gave both of us some jackets and some-- some pants and so forth or whatever. And they were so nice to us.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I said, gee, this is-- this is where the world turns. This is where the-- all-- everything changes now, you know? And I said-- to Kurt, I said, you know what we ought to do is that the Americans are right behind us. We should-- we should try to get on the train and go back to Berlin. That's our best chance, to survive there. And that's what we-- and-- because we didn't-- we didn't expect the American and Allied-- Western Allied troops to stop on the Elbe River. And Berlin is on the east side, way east from the Elbe River. We had no idea what-- what the plans for the end of the war were by the Allied powers. So we get on the train. And it was very dangerous, because we don't really have-- excuse me-- any identification papers. But we managed to get into Berlin. And we jump off that train before it pulls into the final terminal, railroad station in Berlin, because we know there are military police patrolling the platforms, and they're going to check the identity of everybody coming off the train. And we don't have any. Oh, they were still in Berlin?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah. They was, yeah. The Allies-- the American-- that was still all Nazi Germany. So we jumped off the-- as the train rolled real slow through the suburbs, yeah-- through the-- you know, they approached the main station, we jumped off the train. And then he went to his house. He had a house that he lived at, his parents. But they were in Bavaria. They hadn't stayed in the-- in Berlin anymore because of the air raids. They had a nice house in Bavaria. He went-- but he had a-- the house in the suburb. He went there. And I went to another-- the apartment that I had left, when-- before I was sent to the camp. My mother and brother--

speaker: interviewer
text: This [INAUDIBLE]-- the count's?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, yeah, where the-- the count's, the baron's apartment. And I just said, you know, nobody in the house. There were only a few people left, mostly some older people and a couple of young women with children. I just said, I was in the-- I was in the army, and I was just been you know, discharged. Or I gave them some reason that were-- that they wouldn't get suspicious. But then it was still dangerous, because if all-- because that was the days before the Russians attacked the city. And there was-- the street-- the city was crowded you know, with German military personnel of all stripes. I mean, there was everybody-- the SS, you know, army. And then they organized people out of-- they organized a resistance group of people that were senior citizens-- 60 years and older, 75 years old-- gave them rifles, honestly. Put on those air raid warden helmets and expect them to take positions on street corners, and-- honesty, and hold-- and hold off the Russians. Of course, that's total nonsense.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it was-- but it was not a good time for me to go out. Because being a young person, it looked like somebody saw me, they wouldn't-- wouldn't even care if I was [GERMAN]. Now. They would care-- they would more think I'm an AWOL German soldier. And they were looking for them. And they strung them up. They caught people like this, soldiers like this, that had gone AWOL. They strung them up on lampposts. I saw them hanging. So that was not a good idea to go out. I stayed in the-- you know, and it was difficult, because I had hardly anything to eat. So I stayed there, until-- and then on-- How did you survive in the few month-- how long did you stay there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the people in the house helped me. They were-- I-- I-- I got them to believe me that I was discharged and that I was-- because of sickness, for medical reasons. So they had-- were sympathetic to me. They shared with me whatever-- when I needed. And I stayed. Yeah, oh yeah, they were OK. They-- yeah, they didn't know about us. They didn't know that I was a [GERMAN]. I never told them about it, even when we first moved in there, before I was-- you know, called into the camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: So then on April 16th-- I remember that distinctly-- 1945, the Russians had gathered all their forces, about 2 million men, for the final assault on Berlin. That was the price-- the price-- what do you call it? The final price, the most coveted target, you know, the-- and they were the one-- you know, that was the-- the capital of Nazi Germany. And it's going to be a capital again of the new Germany. But it was-- and by-- by bad planning, the Allied-- Americans, British, French-- left the capture of Berlin to the Russians. So they had the-- that was their-- that was their-- their move. So April 16th, they started to come across the river, 2-million-men-strong army. And there was no way. That's-- they went around and circled all Berlin. A few of the Nazi people got away the last day. I remember lookin' for food and going out and really taking a chance. Only we-- when you're 18 years old, you don't realize when there's real danger. I had a bicycle down in the-- in the basement of that house. And I got just cabin fever. I couldn't be any longer in that house. I had to get out. And I'm driving through the deserted streets. The Russians are not here. The Russians are just in the process of approaching the city and circling the city.

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm driving through. And for some reason, I'm driving past the chancellery, where Hitler is. Honestly, I don't know how I got out there. And I-- suddenly, I see SS soldiers loading up files and all kinds of other things on-- on trucks. They were getting ready to evacuate. And some of them got out, because there was still one highway open to the west, that the Russians hadn't cut off yet. And-- but I was crazy doing that. Because they could have-- but they were too busy [LAUGHS] worrying about their own fate, you know, than asking any more questions.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I got back, hid in the cellar with the other rest of the people, and the Battle of Berlin started. It was unbelievable. It was thundering. They had these rocket launchers that have eight or 15 you know, guns on one-- on one-- on one mobile platform-- mobilized platform. And those things, when they go off, they make a hellish noise. I mean, it's just-- deafening. And it's just-- I was-- and those poor guys. One day, I went out to get some bread, because the people-- somebody said there is a bakery still open in the vicinity. And-- and we were out of bread, and people were starving in that basement. I was the only one that was young. And the only other men were real old ones left. And they asked me, would I dare? And I went out and get-- to go to that store, but it was bad. Russian snipers, sharpshooters, were already laying across the big railroad yard. And they were taking aim at anything that moves. They don't know who I am. They think I'm a German soldier, and that's-- you know, that's how the war is. I walk down the street. Totally everything is destroyed. And mortar shells are constantly-- and that's the most dangerous thing, because they're-- from a close distance, they come and have a high curve of-- and then they-- and then they-- and they come straight down. It's not like artillery shells. And I'm-- I'm all by myself on the street. And I see a woman on the other side with a shopping bag. And I'm yelling over to her. I says, is that bakery really open?

speaker: interviewee
text: And she said-- and just at that moment, there's a thump, like a dull noise. And I duck into the house that was all burned out. It was just a shell standing, a big apartment building-- duck in there. And at this I look over. I'm trying to repeat my question, because she didn't answer me. She wasn't there. There was a direct hit by a mortar shell on the other side. And I was so lucky, because I had that big shell of that building. I was on this side. And they were firing over and hit the other side. She's gone.

speaker: interviewer
text: I duck into the house, in the foyer or the lobby of that house, and I see the German soldiers crouching. And now I wasn't afraid anymore, when the Russians are right back there, you know? And I said to him, boy, this was a close call-- something like that in German. And he doesn't answer me. So I'm not paid any attention, because the artillery and the mortar fire continues. Then I turn around. I said, where the hell is-- why isn't he answering me, you know? I look at him. He's dead. Somebody-- some sympathetic soul-- soul pulled him into there so he doesn't lay on the street. Ah.

speaker: interviewee
text: So that was the situation. And then, hours later, Russian tanks rumble up the street outside. We in the basement can hear them rumbling. And a little later, Russian soldiers come in the [INAUDIBLE], in the basement with the-- with their automatic weapons, looking for German soldiers. They really weren't doing any-- weren't really interested in civilians. I had a-- I had to be a little bit worried, because I could have been in the German army, you know? And they could have assumed, hey, he's a-- he's a former German. He just, you know, shed his--

speaker: interviewer
text: Uniform.

speaker: interviewee
text: --uniform. He's now in civilian clothes. But otherwise, they weren't-- they were only looking for--

speaker: interviewer
text: Soldiers.

speaker: interviewee
text: --stragglers of the German army, I mean. And then the next day, I could finally dare to go. Everybody tried to go out and get some fresh air. There's tanks sitting on the parkway, big T-34s. And the-- the man-- and the commanding officer is a very sharp-looking, in a leather outfit-- is a major or something, lieutenant in Russia-- speaks fluent German, had studied-- he was an engineer, he told me, and prior had studied, before the war, at a German university, engineering. And so he-- he was the first one to tell us what the outlook was going to be. He says, don't expect the Americans to come here. He says, this is the [? outer. ?] That was decided that we get, the Russians get, Berlin, get to keep Berlin, which was later changed, so you know, they had the four zones. But he was very, very intelligent, very personable. And not only that-- he saw that we had no food in there. And they had set up a field kitchen in one of the trailers, their-- army trailers, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he says, you're welcome to come up. If you have dishes, you have pots, come up. And they were cooking some hearty food. That's exactly how they-- that's exactly how they treated the people. It was-- it was surprising, because you know, always this hatred against this-- this hostility against the Russian, where that were political-- political situations. But the average Russian wasn't-- was just like any other normal human being, you know? And I remember one day being up in that apartment, shortly after there, where they had-- you know, trying to-- In the baron's apartment?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, the baron's apartment. All the-- everything that-- an artillery shell had hit this from the front, facing the big railroad yard. So that was pretty open. But the kitchen was in the back of the apartment was still-- but everything was so dusty from the explosion, from the tremors of the-- you know, of the artillery fire. And I was there. And I heard somebody coming up with heavy step, heavy boots coming up the stairs. And this is on the fourth floor. And I'm wondering-- you know, this building had an elevator. Of course, there was no electricity, so I'm wondering. I said, who the heck is this going to be? And sure enough, a young Russian soldier-- he looked like a teenager. He couldn't-- he was-- you know, he comes in, and he spots me. And he gets-- he gives me a real grim look. Because, again, he thinks I'm-- I'm a German-- ex-German soldier. So I try to explain that I'm Jewish. And I had learned the word for Jewish in-- for being Jewish-- for Jew in-- in Russian. I could say that, because then one of the older men in the basement of this house was a former White Russian, who had immigrated after World War I to Germany. And he had told me the words.

speaker: interviewer
text: So and he gets still-- later, he was still a little bit on-- on-- apprehensive when I told him and seemed to be angered. And then later, I found out that 'Jew' is a curse word in Russian, a lot of times. Like-- it's like calling somebody an SOB or something worse than that, you know? So I-- but I-- somehow I calmed him down. He didn't speak German. I didn't speak Russian. But he looked so young. So I went-- I went over to the table. I said-- I tried to tell him how old-- find out how old he is and how old I am. So I went over to the table. I had no pencil, no paper, but a big-- big layer of dust on the kitchen table. I go and draw an 18 in there, and going like this. And he points-- same age. So he's real friendly now. And he pulls out tobacco and some-- some tobacco cigarette paper. And he starts you know-- and offers me and he rolls a cigarette for me. That was-- So he didn't hurt you?

speaker: interviewee
text: --was the beginning of a-- of a friend-- of a friendly relation-- you know, of a friendly reception there. Yeah, so I didn't have any-- any problem. But there were worse scenes. I mean, I-- some of the Russian soldiers were coming in and [? coming-- ?] you know, scowling through the basements and looking for women. And they were pulling them out and raping them. And I got real angry about this in that building where we were, because there were a couple of young women. And they came over and they dragged them out. I don't know if they dragged them upstairs or I ran over to the-- there was a Russian command post had been set up in the next-- on the next street. And I ran over there. And I asked to see the commandant or whoever is in charge of the Russian. And he had a-- he had a translator there. So they don't speak Russian, but they had a translator.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he said to me-- I told him about it. And I said, two of your men just come over and pulled some-- and dragged some women away, and they're probably raping them. So he -- and that was not allowed. So he said-- or, officially not allowed. So in order to pacify me, he says, well, I'm going to-- I'm going to send two of my like, MPs over there to straighten it out. They came over, but from-- and they took them away. They did take them away, but I don't think-- those two other soldiers away. But I don't think anything happened to them there.

speaker: interviewee
text: They had --the Russian soldiers had a victory celebration that lasted for eight days after Germany is surrounded, after they captured the city. And they could do anything they wanted-- could loot anything. They could do anything what they wanted. And they could even shoot any civilian. I'm surprised that-- you know, still sometimes I wonder how I came that I was not-- that I was not killed, because they still could have mistaken me for a German soldier-- ex-German soldier.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then you-- you stayed on the there in Berlin?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: You stayed on in Berlin for how long?

speaker: interviewee
text: For-- till the Allied forces moved in, till the British, American, and French forces moved in and took-- and occupied their sectors. Berlin became-- on July 4th, Independence-- American Independence Day-- the Allied forces moved in. I watched them coming into the city, the little military convoys that took over. Then the Russians only kept part of-- the eastern part of-- the eastern part of Berlin. It was divided in two.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you stayed there until--

speaker: interviewee
text: I stayed there till August 3.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did you go?

speaker: interviewee
text: Then I went to Munich to look for my family, because they had lived underground, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.
